completeness.rationale: The triage captured most expected items: CSV import as trigger, special characters, ~200-task list size, encoding error, and macOS/v2.3.x. The manual-vs-auto-save distinction is only implicit (reproduction steps say 'Click Save' but never explicitly asks whether auto-save also crashes). Specific versions (macOS 14.2, v2.3.1) were not pinned down.

accuracy.rationale: The triage correctly identifies the CSV import as the trigger and encoding as the mechanism, which satisfies acceptable diagnostic path 1. The hypothesis that the import path ingests in a legacy encoding is slightly off — the actual bug is in the save serializer panicking on non-ASCII rather than an import-time encoding failure — but the CSV/encoding causal chain is correct and actionable.

thoroughness.rationale: The agent asked good questions and reached a defensible conclusion, but never asked whether auto-save also crashes (a key distinguishing detail listed in expected extracts), and did not pin down the exact app or OS version despite those being straightforward asks. The log-file attempt, while reasonable, was unproductive and didn't recover these gaps.

economy.rationale: Three question turns is efficient, but the log-file request was poorly calibrated: the reporter had already demonstrated limited technical comfort, and the attempt yielded nothing. A more targeted ask (exact version number, or whether auto-save behaves differently) would have been higher value.

question_quality.rationale: The second question was the standout — connecting the encoding flash to special characters from CSV import and offering a low-destructive diagnostic (binary search by deleting half the tasks) showed genuine diagnostic reasoning. The first question was solid triage hygiene. The log-file question was reasonable in concept but miscalibrated to the reporter's technical level, reducing the score slightly.

actionability.rationale: The final summary is exceptionally actionable: it includes a precise root cause hypothesis, step-by-step reproduction instructions with concrete character examples, a three-part recommended fix (immediate crash guard, import sanitization, data migration), a proposed test case, and explicitly enumerated information gaps. A developer could start work immediately.

notable_strengths: Second question demonstrated genuine diagnostic reasoning — connecting the encoding flash to CSV-imported special characters without the reporter volunteering that link
Suggested the binary-search deletion test as a low-friction diagnostic before asking the reporter to touch their data
Final summary is exceptionally developer-ready: reproduction steps, specific character examples, three-tier fix recommendation, proposed test case, and honest information-gap enumeration
notable_weaknesses: Never asked whether auto-save triggers the same crash — a key distinguishing detail that would have directly confirmed the manual-save-specific failure path
Log-file request was poorly calibrated to a self-described non-technical reporter and consumed a full turn yielding no information
Exact version numbers (macOS 14.2, v2.3.1) were never pinned down despite being a straightforward ask
most_insightful_question: Do any of your 200 tasks contain special characters — things like emoji, accented letters, non-English text, or text you copied in from another application? — This directly connected the encoding clue the reporter mentioned to a concrete, testable hypothesis without the reporter having made that link themselves.
missed_opportunities: Ask whether auto-save (if enabled) also causes a crash, which would have confirmed the manual-save-specific trigger
Ask for the exact app version and macOS version upfront alongside platform questions in the first turn
After the reporter confirmed CSV import, ask when exactly the crash started — before or immediately after the import — to establish the causal timeline more precisely
